title: Why the return of feeling your feelings is the hardest and most important work
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

htmlContent:
The moment the numbness breaks

I remember the exact moment I felt my feelings again. Not the dramatic, cathartic release you see in movies. It was a Tuesday afternoon. I was washing dishes. And suddenly, without warning, I felt the weight of my own grief pressing against my ribs like a living thing. I had been numb for so long I forgot what pressure felt like. I dropped a plate. It shattered. And I stood there, surrounded by ceramic shards, sobbing for reasons I couldn't name.

Here's the thing. When you've survived complex trauma, your nervous system doesn't just protect you from danger. It protects you from feeling anything at all. Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote the book on trauma's effects on the body, calls this the "price of survival." Your brain decides that feeling is too expensive. So it shuts down the emotional accounts. You walk through life hollowed out, functional but not alive.

And then, if you do the work, if you build enough safety in your body and your life, the feelings start to return. Not all at once. They trickle in like water through a crack in a dam. A small sadness here. A flash of anger there. And you think, finally, I'm healing. But you're not prepared for what comes next.

What comes next is the flood.

Your nervous system has been storing unprocessed emotion for years, sometimes decades. Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, spent his career watching how animals in the wild shake off trauma and return to regulation. Humans don't get to shake. We hold it. We clench. We brace. We store the unfinished business of our lives in our tissues, our fascia, our breath. And when the dam finally breaks, it's not gentle. It's a tsunami of everything you never let yourself feel.

I'm not telling you this to scare you. I'm telling you because no one warned me. And I think you deserve to know what's coming.

The wellness industry lied to you about feeling

Let me be direct about this. The wellness industry has sold us a version of "feeling your feelings" that looks like a candlelit yoga class where you cry softly into your mat and then go get a matcha latte. That's not what this is. That's not what any of this is.

Real feeling is ugly. It's messy. It's waking up at 3 AM with your heart pounding because your body finally feels safe enough to remember what happened to you. It's driving home from work and suddenly sobbing so hard you have to pull over. It's rage that comes out of nowhere, directed at people who don't deserve it, because the person who does deserve it is long gone or dead or unreachable.

Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist who works extensively with the structural dissociation model, says that "feelings are not the enemy, but they can feel like the enemy when they've been exiled for decades." She's right. The feelings themselves aren't dangerous. But the intensity, the volume, the sheer force of them after years of silence? That can feel like you're dying.

You're not dying. You're coming back to life. And that's harder than dying, honestly. At least death is quiet.

I've had clients tell me they miss the numbness. They miss the dissociation. They miss the fog that made everything feel distant and manageable. And I get it. I really do. The fog was awful, but at least it was predictable. At least it didn't hurt this way. At least they could function.

But here's what I know now that I didn't know then. Functioning isn't living. Getting through the day isn't the same as being present for it. The return of feeling is the return of life itself. And life, as it turns out, is not comfortable. It was never supposed to be.

Right? Think about it. We were sold this idea that healing would make us feel better. And it does, eventually. But first, it makes us feel everything. The good, the bad, the ugly, the boring, the overwhelming, the mundane. All of it. At once. Without the buffer we built to survive.

Your body doesn't know the difference between then and now

This is where the science gets real. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, explains that your nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and danger. It doesn't use your conscious thoughts to make these assessments. It uses your body's cues. Your heart rate. Your breath. The tension in your shoulders. The pitch of someone's voice.

When you start feeling again, your body doesn't know that the feelings are old. It doesn't know that the person who hurt you is gone or that the situation is over. It just knows the signal. And it responds as if the threat is still here, right now, in this moment.

So you're sitting in your living room, safe, warm, maybe with a cup of tea, and your body is screaming at you like you're about to be attacked. That's not a sign that you're broken. That's a sign that your nervous system is doing exactly what it was designed to do. It's just doing it at the wrong time.

Stay with me here. This is the part that matters. The goal isn't to stop these responses. The goal is to teach your body, slowly, carefully, that it's safe now. And you do that by feeling the feelings without acting on them. You sit in the fire without running out of the room. You let the wave pass through you instead of trying to stop it or numb it or fix it.

This is the hardest work I've ever done. Harder than therapy. Harder than medication. Harder than any coping skill I've ever learned. Because it requires me to trust my body after years of not trusting it. It requires me to stay present when every instinct I have is screaming at me to leave.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? by Oprah Winfrey and Dr. Bruce Perry. It helped me understand that my reactions weren't character flaws, they were adaptations. I wasn't broken. I was just doing what I had to do to survive.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving by Pete Walker. He breaks down the specific ways that complex trauma affects emotional regulation and gives you a roadmap for the return of feeling that doesn't pretend it's easy.

The rage you didn't know you had

Let's talk about the feeling no one wants to admit they're feeling. Rage.

When you've been trained to be nice, to be compliant, to be small, to not make waves, the return of anger can feel terrifying. I remember the first time I felt real, undiluted rage in my body. I was 34 years old. I had been in therapy for six years. And I was furious. Not at anything in particular. Just at everything. At the people who hurt me. At the people who didn't protect me. At the world that let it happen. At myself for not being stronger, smarter, faster.

I thought I was regressing. I thought I was getting worse. I called my therapist in a panic and told her I was angry all the time and I didn't know what to do with it.

She said something I will never forget. She said, "Good. The anger means you're starting to believe you deserved better."

Wild, right? The anger isn't a problem. The anger is proof that your sense of self is returning. That somewhere inside you, a part of you knows that what happened wasn't okay. That part is finally strong enough to speak. And it's speaking in the only language it knows, which is rage.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace by Nedra Glover Tawwab. Because here's the thing about rage. It's often a sign that your boundaries were crossed and you didn't have the words or the safety to say no. Learning to set boundaries gives the anger somewhere to go. It gives it a direction. It turns it from a destructive force into a protective one.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts by Richard Schwartz. He's the creator of Internal Family Systems, and his work helped me understand that the rage wasn't the whole of me. It was a part of me. A part that had been protecting me for a long time. A part that deserved compassion, not condemnation.

The grief that has no bottom

And then there's the grief. The grief that comes when you realize what you lost. The years you spent dissociating. The relationships you couldn't sustain. The version of yourself that never got to exist because you were too busy surviving.

This grief doesn't have a bottom. I've been grieving for years now, and I'm still finding new layers. New losses I didn't know I was carrying. New parts of myself that I never got to meet.

Dan Siegel, the psychiatrist who coined the term "mindsight," talks about how integration is the key to healing. Integration means bringing together the parts of yourself that got split off. And that requires grieving. Grieving the childhood you didn't have. Grieving the safety you never felt. Grieving the love that wasn't there when you needed it most.

There's no shortcut through grief. No hack. No protocol. You just have to sit in it. Let it wash over you. Let it take as long as it takes.

And here's the part no one tells you. The grief doesn't go away. It changes. It becomes less sharp. Less consuming. But it doesn't disappear. You learn to carry it. You learn to make room for it. You learn to live alongside it.

I used to think that was depressing. Now I think it's honest. And honesty, in this work, is worth more than any promise of a pain-free life.

You will not be the same person on the other side

This is the last thing I want to tell you. And I need you to hear it clearly.

The return of feeling will change you. It will change who you are. It will change what you can tolerate. It will change the relationships you're willing to stay in and the ones you have to leave. It will change your career, your hobbies, your sense of humor, your taste in music. It will change everything.

And you won't be able to go back. Once you start feeling, really feeling, you can't unfeel. You can't stuff it all back in the box. The box is broken. The dam is gone. The numbness is a door that only opens one way.

I'm not saying this to scare you. I'm saying it because I wish someone had said it to me. I wish someone had told me that healing wouldn't make me more comfortable. It would make me more alive. And being alive, truly alive, is the most uncomfortable thing I've ever done.

But it's also the only thing worth doing.

So if you're in the middle of the flood right now. If the feelings are coming and you don't know how to hold them. If you're wondering if you're getting worse instead of better. I see you. I've been there. I'm still there, some days.

Keep going. The feelings won't kill you. They feel like they will, but they won't. And on the other side of them, there's a life that's actually yours. A life where you're not just surviving. You're feeling. You're present. You're here.

And that, my friend, is everything.

Healing Journey Resources

  • What Happened to You? by Oprah Winfrey and Dr. Bruce Perry - A compassionate reframing of trauma that helps you understand your reactions as adaptations, not flaws.
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving by Pete Walker - A guide to the specific challenges of complex trauma and emotional regulation.
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace by Nedra Glover Tawwab - A practical guide to boundaries that can help channel anger into protection.
  • No Bad Parts by Richard Schwartz - A gentle introduction to parts work and Internal Family Systems.

As an Amazon Associate, I earn from qualifying purchases.
